attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine check-up. She has a medical history of obesity and type 2 diabetes, managed with metformin. She does not take any other regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index (BMI) is 32 kg/m2. Recent blood tests show elevated fasting insulin levels, indicating insulin resistance.

In the context of obesity-related chronic kidney disease, insulin resistance can lead to increased insulin secretion. This can result in podocyte and glomerular filtration barrier modulation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the next step in this pathway that leads to kidney injury and fibrosis?
attributes.explanation:
In the pathway of obesity-related chronic kidney disease, insulin resistance leads to increased insulin secretion, which can modulate podocytes and the glomerular filtration barrier (GFB). This modulation results in albuminuria, which is the leakage of albumin into the urine due to increased permeability of the GFB. Albuminuria is a key early marker of kidney damage and can lead to further kidney injury and fibrosis if not addressed.

Glomerular hyperfiltration is an early compensatory mechanism in response to increased metabolic demands but is not directly the next step following podocyte and GFB modulation.

Inflammation and oxidative stress are involved in the progression of kidney damage but occur downstream of albuminuria.

Renin-angiotensin system activation can contribute to kidney damage and hypertension but is not the immediate next step following podocyte and GFB modulation.

Volume overload and hypertension are consequences of kidney damage but are not directly linked to the immediate effects of podocyte and GFB modulation.
learningPoints: Obesity can lead to chronic kidney disease through pathways involving insulin resistance and hyperinsulinaemia.
Podocyte and glomerular filtration barrier modulation can lead to albuminuria, a key step in kidney injury and fibrosis.
Albuminuria is an early marker of kidney damage and can progress to chronic kidney disease if not addressed.
references: Nawaz S, Chinnadurai R, Al-Chalabi S, Evans P, Kalra PA, Syed AA, Sinha S. Obesity and chronic kidney disease: A current review. Obes Sci Pract. 2022 Jul 19;9(2):61-74. doi: 10.1002/osp4.629. PMID: 37034567; PMCID: PMC10073820.

Jacob P, McCafferty K. Assessment and management of chronic kidney disease in people living with obesity. Clin Med (Lond). 2023 Jul;23(4):353-356. doi: 10.7861/clinmed.2023-0195. PMID: 37524431; PMCID: PMC10541045.
Hojs R, Ekart R, Bevc S, Vodošek Hojs N. Chronic Kidney Disease and Obesity. Nephron. 2023;147(11):660-664. doi: 10.1159/000531379. Epub 2023 Jun 2. PMID: 37271131.

Jiang Z, Wang Y, Zhao X, Cui H, Han M, Ren X, Gang X, Wang G. Obesity and chronic kidney disease. Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab. 2023 Jan 1;324(1):E24-E41. doi: 10.1152/ajpendo.00179.2022. Epub 2022 Nov 16. PMID: 36383637.
